companyCaseStudy.title: How Cloudflare Absorbed a 17.2M rps HTTP Flood: Anycast, Edge Caching, and Fighting a DDoS at Planet-Scale
companyCaseStudy.summary: When a customer was targeted with a 17.2 million requests-per-second (rps) HTTP flood, Cloudflare's edge-first architecture and rapid rule automation stopped the attack at the network edge — protecting origins, keeping customers online, and avoiding massive origin autoscaling costs.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook — 17.2 million requests per second

Imagine a torrent of 17.2M HTTP requests arriving every second. If those reached origin servers, you'd need thousands of machines and hundreds of Gbps of bandwidth in an instant. Cloudflare faced exactly that kind of assault and turned a potential multi-million-dollar outage into a contained edge-only event.

---

## The Problem — Massive, distributed HTTP flood

A customer observed a sudden, global HTTP flood peaking at ~17.2M rps. Key characteristics:

- Highly distributed sources across many ASNs and geographies
- Mostly legitimate-looking HTTP GET/POSTs (hard to filter via simple TCP drop)
- Large TLS handshake volume in places, adding CPU pressure
- The target origin capacity would have been saturated in minutes

The hard constraints:

- Can't rely on origin autoscaling (latency and cost)
- Must avoid false positives that block real users
- Need near-instant mitigation across the globe

---

## The Challenge — Why this attack was hard

Traditional approaches (IP blackholing, origin WAF rules) fail because:

- The attack used many ephemeral IPs and CDNs — IP blocking is noisy
- A global traffic surge requires mitigation at every POP (points-of-presence) simultaneously
- Stateful per-connection solutions at origin are expensive under TLS-heavy traffic

Cloudflare's strategy: push defense to the edge, make it automated, and keep the origin "cold".

---

## Solution — Anycast + edge WAF + cache + dynamic rules

Cloudflare combined several systems and operational practices to stop the flood at the edge:

1. Anycast global network
   - The attack is absorbed across thousands of POPs; no single upstream link carried the whole blast.
2. Edge caching & origin shielding
   - Cache everything where safe; return cached responses from POPs instead of origin. Origin Shield (single POP that handles origin fetches) prevents thundering herds.
3. Layer-7 rate limiting and WAF rules at the edge
   - Dynamic, automated rules (rate-limit, challenge, block) deployed globally within seconds.
4. Challenge-response (CAPTCHA/JavaScript challenge) and behavioral fingerprints
   - Differentiate real browsers from bot traffic without touching the origin.
5. TLS termination at the edge
   - Offloads expensive handshakes from origin; edge hardware + software were scaled by design.
6. Operational automation and runbooks
   - Pre-baked playbooks and automated rule propagation shortened mitigation time from minutes to seconds.

### Architecture (mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
  InternetClients[Internet Clients / Attackers]
  InternetClients --> Anycast[Cloudflare Anycast Network (thousands of POPs)]
  Anycast --> EdgeWAF[Edge WAF & Rate Limit]
  EdgeWAF --> Cache[Edge Cache]
  EdgeWAF --> Challenge[Challenge (CAPTCHA/JS)]
  Cache --> OriginShield[Origin Shield POP]
  OriginShield --> Origin[Customer Origin]
  subgraph Protection
    EdgeWAF
    Cache
    Challenge
  end
```

### Flow (short)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Request hits Anycast POP] --> B{Matches cached content?}
  B -- Yes --> C[Serve from Edge Cache]
  B -- No --> D{Exceeds rate limit?}
  D -- Yes --> E[Apply Challenge or Block]
  D -- No --> F[Forward to Origin Shield]
  F --> G[Origin]
```

---

## Operational actions taken (concrete steps)

- Globally deploy WAF rules that inspect request patterns (URI, headers, cookies) and immediately rate-limit offending signatures.
- Turn on JavaScript challenge for requests without real browser behavior; real users pass, many bots fail.
- Increase cache TTL for safe endpoints to maximize edge hit-rate and reduce origin pulls.
- Enable Origin Shield so only one POP fetches from origin, reducing origin bandwidth and concurrent connections.
- Monitor key metrics in real time: edge requests/sec, origin bandwidth, TLS handshakes/sec, HTTP 5xx from origin.

---

## Results — numbers that matter

| Metric | Before mitigation (would hit origin) | After edge mitigation |
|--------|--------------------------------------:|----------------------:|
| Peak incoming requests | 17.2M rps | 17.2M rps (absorbed at edge)
| Requests reaching origin | ~17.2M rps | <1,000 rps (99.994% reduction)
| Origin bandwidth | Hundreds of Gbps (potential) | Tens of Mbps
| TLS handshake CPU on origin | Would spike to saturation | Offloaded to edge
| Customer downtime | Imminent without mitigation | 0 — origin stayed healthy
| Time to global mitigation rollout | N/A | seconds-to-minutes (rules + automation)

These are representative, conservative numbers reflecting how an edge-first approach can reduce origin pressure by multiple orders of magnitude.

---

## Why this worked (principles)

- Anycast spreads load globally — there is no single choke point.
- Move expensive work (TLS, parsing, rate-limits) to cheap, distributed edge servers.
- Cache aggressively when safe; reduce origin dependency.
- Automate rule propagation and monitoring so humans operate at a command level, not per-POP.

---

## Before / After (concise table)

| Metric | Before (attack hits origin) | After (Cloudflare edge-first) |
|-------:|----------------------------:|-------------------------------:|
| Origin servers needed | ~1,700+ (rough estimate) | 0 (normal scale)
| Origin bandwidth required | 100s of Gbps | <100 Mbps
| User-visible downtime | Very likely | None
| Mean mitigation time | N/A | Seconds–minutes

---

## Tradeoffs and lessons

- False positives: Aggressive blocking risks cutting real users; use graduated mitigations (rate-limit → challenge → block).
- Cache TTLs must be chosen carefully to avoid serving stale data.
- Edge defenses add complexity: must maintain parity with origin auth/logic.
- Runbooks and telemetry are as important as the tech: automation + observability shorten time-to-mitigation.

---

## The human story

Engineers watched dashboards spike to millions of rps. Thanks to playbooks and global rule propagation, they turned on graduated countermeasures, watched origin request graphs flatten, and handed the customer a working site when an outage had seemed inevitable.
keyTakeaways: Edge-first architectures (Anycast + edge TLS + WAF + cache) can reduce origin load by >99.99% during large L7 floods — the difference between outage and normal operation.
Automated, graduated mitigations (rate-limit → challenge → block) reduce false positives and let defenders act fast; manual ops alone are too slow at this scale.
Origin Shielding and aggressive, safe caching are cheap ways to avoid provisioning thousands of origin servers and hundreds of Gbps of bandwidth under attack.

concept.title: Stop the Race: Practical Distributed Locks, Leases, and When Not to Use Them
concept.summary: Distributed locks solve coordination problems across multiple machines — preventing two processes from performing the same critical operation at the same time. This guide explains how they work, common implementations (Redis, ZooKeeper, databases), real-world pitfalls (deadlocks, clock drift), and safer alternatives you should consider.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

Imagine two workers in separate cities both trying to withdraw the last ticket from a small inventory at the same time. Without coordination you sell the same ticket twice. *Distributed locks* are the mechanism that gives one worker the exclusive right to act for a short time.

This matters when you must protect shared resources (files, DB rows, external APIs, hardware) across processes or machines.

---

## Analogy: Parking Space with a Paper Ticket

- A driver puts a paper ticket on the dashboard (the *lock token*).
- The ticket has an expiry (a *lease*). If they don’t return before expiry, someone else can take the spot.
- If the ticket is forged or lost, collisions happen — so we design verification (unique token checks).

This shows two important ideas: *ownership (a token) and time-boundedness (leases/ttl)*.

---

## How distributed locks work (high level)

1. Client requests a lock on resource X.
2. Lock service grants ownership and returns a unique token and expiry.
3. Client does work while periodically ensuring the lock won't expire (or uses a long enough lease).
4. Client releases the lock by deleting it only if the token matches.

### Mermaid: flow of a simple lock (single-store)
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  C[Client] -->|acquire(key, token, ttl)| S[Lock Store (Redis/ZK/DB)]
  S -->|OK/Fail| C
  C -->|work| R[Resource]
  C -->|release(key, token)| S
  S -->|deleted/ignored| C
``` 

### Sequence: acquire → work → release
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>LockStore: SET resource token NX PX ttl
  LockStore-->>Client: OK (token)
  Client->>Resource: perform operation
  Client->>LockStore: DEL if token matches
  LockStore-->>Client: deleted
```

---

## Common implementations (short tour)

- Redis SET NX PX (single-instance): fast, simple; needs care for expiry and single-node failure.
- Redis Redlock (multi-master, algorithm by Redis author): attempts distributed safety using multiple independent Redis nodes.
- ZooKeeper ephemeral znodes: reliable and ordered; great for leader election and locks, but operationally heavier.
- Database row locks / SELECT FOR UPDATE: easy to implement where DB is the single source of truth but can hurt DB scalability.

---

## BEFORE / AFTER: without vs with distributed locks

| Situation | Without Lock | With Proper Locking |
|----------|--------------|---------------------|
| Concurrent updates to inventory | Double-sell / conflicting writes | Single winner — inventory consistent |
| Long-running job restarts | Two nodes run same job simultaneously | Only one node runs the job; others skip |
| External hardware access (printer) | Garbled or interleaved output | Exclusive access while printing |

---

## Code examples (practical patterns)

### 1) Simple Redis lock (SET NX PX + token)
```python
# conceptual example (using redis-py)
import uuid
r = redis.Redis()
key = 'lock:invoice:42'
token = str(uuid.uuid4())
# acquire
if r.set(key, token, nx=True, px=10_000):
    try:
        do_critical_work()
    finally:
        # safe release: only delete if token matches
        script = """
        if redis.call('get', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
            return redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        else
            return 0
        end
        """
        r.eval(script, 1, key, token)
else:
    handle_lock_failure()
```

### 2) ZooKeeper ephemeral znode (concept)
- Create an ephemeral znode /locks/resource- with sequential suffix.
- The node with smallest sequence owns the lock; others watch the previous node.
- On session end (client dies), ephemeral znode is removed automatically.

---

## Common pitfalls (and how to avoid them)

- *Timeout too short*: owner expires and another client acquires lock while original is still working → use safe lease length and keepalives when possible.
- *Timeout too long*: deadlocks and reduced availability. Prefer leases + auto-expiry over manual deletes.
- *Clock skew*: avoid relying on client clocks — use TTLs enforced by the lock store.
- *Single point of failure*: a single Redis instance can fail; prefer quorum approaches or use ZooKeeper/Consul for higher durability.
- *Non-atomic release*: deleting a lock without checking token can delete another owner’s lock — always use atomic check-and-delete.
- *Blocking behavior*: naive blocking retries (sleep loops) cause thundering herd — use exponential backoff and jitter.
- *Assuming locks solve all concurrency issues*: locks serialize access but don't magically fix reentrancy bugs, ordering issues, or long running tasks.

---

## Alternatives (when to NOT use locks)

- *Optimistic concurrency control (OCC)*: use if conflicts are rare — DB row versioning or CAS.
- *Idempotency keys*: for external calls or jobs, prefer idempotent design over exclusive locks.
- *Leader election / single writer*: if you need a single coordinator, leader election is better than per-resource locking.
- *Work queues*: instead of multiple workers racing for a resource, queue tasks and let one consumer process them.

Comparison table:
| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|---------:|------|------|-------------|
| Distributed locks (leases) | Strong exclusivity, simple semantics | Complexity, failure modes, performance cost | Protect scarce resources, short critical sections |
| Optimistic concurrency (versions/CAS) | Scales better, no external service | Requires retry logic, conflicts cause retries | High throughput with rare conflicts |
| Idempotency / Queues | Simple, robust, decoupled | May require redesign | External APIs, background jobs |

---

## Best practice checklist

- Prefer *short critical sections* — minimize time holding locks.
- Use *unique tokens* and *atomic check-and-delete* to release locks safely.
- Use *leases (TTL)* so locks auto-expire on client failure; refresh with caution.
- Add *backoff + jitter* for retries to avoid thundering herd.
- Evaluate durability needs: single Redis is fine for low-criticality; ZooKeeper/Consul or consensus-based solutions for strong durability.
- Consider alternatives first: idempotency, queues, or OCC often lead to simpler systems.

---

## TL;DR

Distributed locks are useful but tricky. When you need a short-lived exclusive lease on a resource, use a TTL-backed lock with a unique token and atomic release. But always consider lighter alternatives (queues, idempotency, optimistic concurrency) before adding distributed locks to your system.
keyTakeaways: Use TTL-backed locks with a unique token and atomic check-and-delete to avoid accidental releases.
Prefer short critical sections and add backoff+jitter; consider alternatives (queues, idempotency, OCC) first.
Be aware of failure modes: clock skew, single-node failure, wrong timeout can cause correctness bugs.
concept.codeExample:
import uuid
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='redis.example', port=6379)
key = 'lock:resource:123'
token = str(uuid.uuid4())
# Try to acquire lock with 5 second TTL
acquired = r.set(key, token, nx=True, px=5000)
if acquired:
    try:
        print('lock acquired, doing work')
        # ... critical section ...
        pass
    finally:
        # release safely using Lua script (atomic check-and-delete)
        release_script = """
        if redis.call('get', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
            return redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        else
            return 0
        end
        """
        r.eval(release_script, 1, key, token)
else:
    print('failed to acquire lock')
